coredata.dc:title: Microfluid biosensor for detection of hpv in patient scraping samples: Determining E6 and E7 oncogenes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Oliveira, Léony S.
Goel, Sanket
Amreen, Khairunnisa
Lucena-Silva, Norma
Oliveira, Maria D.L.
Andrade, Cesar A.S.
coredata.dc:description: E6 and E7 oncogenes are pivotal in the carcinogenic transformation in HPV infections and efficient diagnostic methods can ensure the detection and differentiation of HPV genotype. This study describes the development and validation of an electrochemical, label-free genosensor coupled with a microfluidic system for detecting the E6 and E7 oncogenes in cervical scraping samples. The nanostructuring employed was based on a cysteine and graphene quantum dots layer that provides functional groups, surface area, and interesting electrochemical properties. Biorecognition tests with cervical scraping samples showed differentiation in the voltammetric response. Low-risk HPV exhibited a lower biorecognition response, reflected in ΔI% values of 82.33 % ± 0.29 for HPV06 and 80.65 % ± 0.68 for HPV11 at a dilution of 1:100. Meanwhile, high-risk, HPV16 and HPV18, demonstrated ΔI% values of 96.65 % ± 1.27 and 93 % ± 0.026, respectively, at the same dilution. Therefore, the biorecognition intensity followed the order: HPV16 >HPV18 >HPV06 >HPV11. The limit of detection and the limit of quantification of E6E7 microfluidic LOC−Genosensor was 26 fM, and 79.6 fM. Consequently, the E6E7 biosensor is a valuable alternative for clinical HPV diagnosis, capable of detecting the potential for oncogenic progression even in the early stages of infection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microfluidic
HPV
Graphene quantum dots
Genosensor
E6E7

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization by atomic microscopy and scanning electron microscopy
Electrochemical characterization of the self-assembly steps

L\u00E9ony S. Oliveira would like to thank CAPES and CNPq for the scholarship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Development and Validation Stages of the E6E7 microfluidic lab-on-chip genosensor.

Atomic force microscopy study of of the assembly and bio-recognition steps of the microfluidic lab-on-chip genosensor, where: (a) Cys, (b) Cys-GQD, (c) Cys-GQD-E6E7Probe, (d) Cys-GQD-E6E7Probe-HPV16, (e) Cys-GQD-E6E7Probe-HPV11 and (f) Cys-GQD-E6E7Probe-Negative control.
E6E7 Microfluidic lab-on-chip genosensor construction steps characterized by cyclic voltammetry (a) and differential pulse voltammetry (b).

Electrochemical characterization by DPV (a) and anodic peak currents (b) biorecognition tests of the E6E7 Microfluidic lab-on-chip genosensor against plasmid samples of HPV06, 11, 16, 18, 33, and 45 (10 pg/µL). DPV of the linear assay with HPV33 plasmid (c) and the respective calibration curve (d) at different plasmid concentrations (1, 5, 10, 15, and 20 pg/µL) (linear equation: y = 86.08 + 5.66 log (×)).

Electrochemical characterization by DPV of the biorecognition of the E6E7 Microfluidic lab-on-chip genosensor against clinical cervical scraping samples positive for HPV06 (a), HPV11 (b), HPV16 (c), HPV18 (d) (1:100–1:300).
3D plot of the recognition process of the E6E7 microfluidic lab-on-chip genosensor against HPV06, 11, 16, and 18.
Comparative study of the E6E7 microfluidic lab-on-chip genosensor with other works for HPV detection on carbon electrodes.

head.ce:title: Microfluid biosensor for detection of hpv in patient scraping samples: Determining E6 and E7 oncogenes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: E6 and E7 oncogenes are pivotal in the carcinogenic transformation in HPV infections and efficient diagnostic methods can ensure the detection and differentiation of HPV genotype. This study describes the development and validation of an electrochemical, label-free genosensor coupled with a microfluidic system for detecting the E6 and E7 oncogenes in cervical scraping samples. The nanostructuring employed was based on a cysteine and graphene quantum dots layer that provides functional groups, surface area, and interesting electrochemical properties. Biorecognition tests with cervical scraping samples showed differentiation in the voltammetric response. Low-risk HPV exhibited a lower biorecognition response, reflected in ΔI% values of 82.33 % ± 0.29 for HPV06 and 80.65 % ± 0.68 for HPV11 at a dilution of 1:100. Meanwhile, high-risk, HPV16 and HPV18, demonstrated ΔI% values of 96.65 % ± 1.27 and 93 % ± 0.026, respectively, at the same dilution. Therefore, the biorecognition intensity followed the order: HPV16 >HPV18 >HPV06 >HPV11. The limit of detection and the limit of quantification of E6E7 microfluidic LOC−Genosensor was 26 fM, and 79.6 fM. Consequently, the E6E7 biosensor is a valuable alternative for clinical HPV diagnosis, capable of detecting the potential for oncogenic progression even in the early stages of infection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Human Papillomavirus (HPV) is a sexually transmitted virus responsible for mucosal infections in anogenital and oral regions, with a high global incidence. HPV is classified by its oncogenic risk into low-risk genotypes (LR-HPV) and high-risk genotypes (HR-HPV), with the high-risk types being the primary ones associated with cervical cancer development [1,2]. In 2020, there were an estimated 604,127 cases of cervical cancer worldwide, with a mortality rate of 56.58 %. Therefore, distinguishing oncogenic risk is crucial for medical decisions regarding treatment [3].

The E6 and E7 oncogenes produce proteins that are fundamental to the HPV life cycle, especially in infection and cell transformation stages. E6 inhibits apoptosis by destroying the p53 protein, preventing abnormal cell growth. On the other hand, E7 stimulates cell division by inactivating the Rb (retinoblastoma) protein, which controls the cell cycle. Thus, E6 and E7 proteins are essential in forming precursor lesions that can lead to cervical cancer development [2,4,5]. Besides HPV genotype differentiation, the early determination of oncogenic markers can significantly affect clinical decisions.

The primary diagnosis of HPV is based on colposcopy examination with visualizations of cervical lesions and the Papanicolaou test, where cytological changes characteristic of viral infection is observed. Despite the initial sensitivity of the Papanicolaou test in detecting HPV infection, it lacks specificity [6]. Therefore, molecular techniques like in situ hybridization and RT-qPCR are the preferred options for viral genotyping. While efficient, these techniques require a controlled laboratory environment and specialized professionals for execution. Additionally, both methods involve extensive protocols and can result in false positives due to sample impurities [1,7].

Genosensors are genetic assessment biosensors that reliably identify genetic markers with straightforward protocols. Furthermore, these tools offer high specificity and miniaturization capabilities [8]. However, the real-world application of portable and miniaturized devices as point-of-care solutions remains challenging. Different technologies, such as bioelectronics and microfluidics, are integrated into their configuration to enhance the bioanalytical performance of these devices. Microfluidic systems are based on the operation and flow of small volumes of fluids on a micrometric scale. Microchannels allow precise control of reactions occurring on the substrate, which is valuable when combined with electrochemical transduction systems, allowing the development of lab-on-chip systems [9].

Voltammetric techniques are widely used in developing electrochemical genosensors due to their ease of signal acquisition and interpretation. Moreover, there is a wide range of voltammetry variations, with the most commonly used being cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV) [10]. The integration of microfluidics and electrochemistry into biosensors enables a broad range of innovative devices to detect breast cancer oncomarkers [11] and viral detection [12], among other applications.

Furthermore, with the expansion of molecular bionanotechnology, biological molecules, and innovative nanomaterials can be applied to construct self-assembled sensor platforms. Cysteine (Cys) is a frequently used amino acid in biosensor construction due to its chemical groups, including NH, COOH, and SH, which can be leveraged for immobilization strategies [13]. Additionally, Cys exhibits significant electrochemical behavior and can be integrated into the electrode surfaces of various materials, such as gold [14] and carbon [15].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are innovative nanocrystals with interesting optical and electronic properties associated with biocompatibility and non-toxicity. Electrochemically, GQDs have high charge transfer capabilities and redox reactions, allowing for analytical detection and quantification. When applied to self-assembled layer formation, GQDs provide chemical groups like COOH and OH, enabling biomolecule immobilization without losing their conformation and biological activity. Furthermore, this nanomaterial increases the surface area, ensuring greater immobilization of molecules and forming an organized and biocompatible layer [15,16].

This work presents the development and validation of an electrochemical DNA biosensor integrated with a microfluidic system for detecting the E6 and E7 oncogenes related to HPV infection. A nanostructured layer of Cys and GQDs was used to immobilize a specific DNA probe to detect E6 and E7 oncogenes, as shown in Fig. 1 .

Sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) and sodium phosphate dibasic dihydrate (Na2HPO4·H2O), N-ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide, N-hydroxysuccinimide, tris base, ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), cysteine (97 %), and GQDs (1 mg/mL) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The specific probe for detecting the HPV E6 and E7 oncogenes was obtained from Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Nuclease-free water was acquired from Thermo Fisher.

Initially, the carbon electrodes were designed using software commonly used for printed circuit board (PCB) applications, and the model was adapted for inkjet printing. In this way, the three electrodes were printed onto a glass substrate. After printing, the electrodes were placed in a hot air oven for 2 h to dry. Subsequently, the reference electrode was created using Ag/AgCl ink with the assistance of a microneedle and left to dry in ambient conditions for 12 h. This process for obtaining the electrode system has been described in more detail in previous works [17–19]. The channel mold was designed using design software (AutoCAD) to create the microchannel and microfluidic system. The pattern was transferred to a glass substrate through direct laser writing photopolymerization. After that, it was placed in a container and filled with PDMS to obtain the pattern. Finally, the ultimate device was heated for 2 h at 70 °C. These steps were based on previous works [19,20]. Finally, fluid input and output ports were used to introduce and remove electrode modification solutions, analytes, and cleaning solutions.

To assemble the E6E7 biosensor initially, a microchannel system and electrodes were employed as the construction environment. A test was carried out to choose the ideal concentration for Cys immobilization with 10-, 20-, 30-, and 40-mM solutions for 5 min as the first modification step. The microchannel was filled with 20 µL of cysteine solution, previously diluted in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). After 5 min, the microchannel was washed with a PBS solution to remove weakly adsorbed molecules. In the subsequent stage, the Cys-functionalized surface was treated with an EDC/NHS solution (0.1 M and 0.4 M, respectively, v/v) for 5 min. Subsequently, the ideal immobilization time of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (0.01 mg/µL) was studied for 1, 5, 10, and 15 min. A fresh microchannel wash with a PBS solution was performed to ensure the removal of unbound residues.

For the formation of the third layer on the Cys-GQDs, an EDC/NHS solution was employed for 5 min, followed by the introduction of the E6E7 probe (25 pM) for a 15-minute interval. The E6E7 probe features an amine group at its end, allowing for its covalent immobilization on the self-assembled surface. Finally, the microchannel underwent one last wash with PBS, and the resulting E6E7 biosensor proceeded to the subsequent validation stages. All construction stages were monitored using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV).

The specific probe for detecting the HPV E6 and E7 oncogene was acquired from Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, CA, USA). For the detection of the oncogenes of interest, a degenerate DNA probe was used to enable the detection of both oncogenes in the same oligonucleotide sequence. The E6E7 reverse probe features the sequence 3′ACNDNRAACCANCCNBDRCA5′ and was used at a concentration of 25 pM. The letters N, D, R, and B in the probe sequence represent the possible nucleotide substitutions standardized by the IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry), where N=any of the four bases (Adenine–A, Guanine–G, Thymine–T, or Cytosine–C), D=A, G, or T, R=a purine (A or G), and B=C, G, or T. Recombinant plasmid samples containing genetic information from different types of HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 33, and 45 were obtained through subcloning into a pTA vector. Oligonucleotide primers (complementary to the vector plasmid binding region) were used for sequencing the recombinant plasmids. The negative control sample was prepared as a solution containing cDNA from the hepatitis C virus and Candida albicans, each at a concentration of 100 pg/µL. All sample dilutions and ssDNAs were prepared in Tris-EDTA buffer solution (pH 8.0; 10 mM) in nuclease-free water from Thermo Fisher and stored frozen. Clinical cervical scraping samples were collected with the patient's consent, and the local ethics committee approved the corresponding protocol (protocol number CAAE 23698513.0.0000.5190).

For the biorecognition tests, cervical scraping samples were diluted in 0.1 M PBS on the day of the test to preserve the target genetic material. Cell suspensions underwent vigorous agitation and disruption through sonication to simplify the exposure of the genetic content from clinical samples. In the hybridization tests, plasmid samples and real clinical samples were heated to 90 °C for one minute for DNA denaturation and then dripped onto the surface of the E6E7 biosensor. Finally, after 20 min, the surface was rinsed with 0.1 M PBS to remove weakly adsorbed molecules and characterized electrochemically.

The potentiostat/galvanostat Autolab PGSTAT128 N with NOVA11.1 software (Metrohm Autolab, Netherlands) was employed as the workstation for electrochemical measurements, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The electrode and microfluidic system served as the electrochemical cell, comprising three carbon electrodes, with the reference electrode coated with silver paste. A 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution was used as an electroanalytical solution. The solution's pH was adjusted to 7.4 using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide. CV experiments were conducted in the potential range of −0.2 to + 0.7 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. DPV experiments were performed in the potential range of −0.198 to + 0.8 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The peak currents were obtained automatically using the same signal acquisition software, NOVA 1.11. The process involves determining the baseline and calculating the current before and after the potential. For the bar graphs, the mean and standard deviation were calculated from the experiments conducted at each testing stage.

Reproducibility in the E6E7 biosensor was calculated by determining the percentage value of the standard deviation for three independent biosensors constructed following the same experimental procedures. Repetition was estimated by considering the relative standard deviation of successive electrochemical measurements in the biorecognition test at a 15 pg/µL concentration of oligonucleotides. The formula used was RSD (%)=(Standard deviation / Average) × 100.

The analysis was carried out utilizing an atomic force microscope (AFM) model SPM-9500 (Shimadzu, Japan). Three-dimensional representations were generated with a silicon cantilever coated with aluminum, resonating at 75 kHz and applying a constant force of 3 Nm−1 (Multi 75AL, NCHR). These measurements were conducted in a non-contact mode at approximately 25 °C. The analysis encompassed a 5 × 5 µm2 area with a raster size of 512 × 512 pixels, and data processing was performed using Gwyddion Software.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was utilized to visualize the assembly of each construction step and observe the nanostructuring of the E6E7 microfluidic biosensor. The microscopy equipment used was the JSM 5600LV model (JEOL), featuring an acceleration voltage of up to 15 kV and magnification of up to 100,000 ×. It was equipped with secondary electron and backscattered electron detectors for surface analysis. This evaluation was conducted using the Scanning Electron Microscopy Platform at the Aggeu Magalhães Institute (FIOCRUZ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization by atomic microscopy and scanning electron microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: In the AFM characterization, the maximum surface peaks and 3D topography of each construction and bio-recognition step of the E6E7/LOC-Genosensor were evaluated. In this study, the peaks, valleys, and roughness features in the images were assessed during the self-assembly and validation of the biosensor. In Fig. 2 a, a granular topography with a maximum peak of 22 nm corresponding to the cysteine layer is observed, with a roughness average (Ra) of 3.03 nm and root mean square (RMS) of 4.12 nm. This behavior indicates the formation of a Cys layer covering the surface [21]. With the immobilization of graphene quantum dots, there was an elevation of 8 nm in the maximum surface peak with Ra = 3.85 nm and RMS= 5.17 nm (Fig. 2b). This elevation is due to the formation of GQD aggregates on the dry surface [16,22]. Upon immobilization of the DNA probe, there was a 13 nm increase in the maximum surface peak, with Ra = 3.98 nm and RMS= 6.74 nm, respectively (Fig. 2c). The increase in surface roughness with probe immobilization is a characteristic associated with the DNA probe binding to the substrate [23]. With the exposure of the sensor layer to the clinical sample of HPV16, the maximum surface peak was 61 nm, demonstrating an increase of 18 nm compared to the sensor layer. Simultaneously, the surface roughness also increased, reflecting Ra = 17.40 nm and RMS= 22.12 nm (Fig. 2d). This behavior indicates the formation of hybridization sites resulting from bio-recognition, and the elevation of the surface peak is associated with the double helix of DNA [24]. The double helix structure presents an inflexible configuration, demonstrating a perpendicular disposition [24]. The same bio-recognition characteristic was observed in Fig. 2e, where the surface showed a maximum height of 55 nm when the sensor layer was exposed to clinical samples of HPV11 (Ra = 8.31 nm and RMS= 12.61 nm). However, when the sensor was exposed to a negative control sample formed by non-complementary cDNA to the E6E7 probe, there was not such a significant increase in the maximum surface height, which remained at 48 nm, with Ra = 5.77 nm and RMS= 7.61 nm. Thus, it is possible to perceive that the sensor structure occurred satisfactorily, where the self-layers showed sensitivity for the detection of positive samples as well as differentiation from the negative sample. In addition, within the positive samples, a possible distinction between HPV16, a high-risk oncogenic type, and HPV11, low risk, can be perceived. This is evident because HR-HPV showed a higher maximum surface peak, Ra, and RMS, potentially indicating more hybridization points than LR-HPV.

In the SEM evaluation of the Cys layer, small surface aggregation points were observed (Fig. S1a). With the adsorption of GQDs through the cross-linking agent EDC/NHS, the surface coating with quantum dots exhibiting a hexagonal shape was observed (Fig. S1b). With the immobilization of the DNA probe, a few modifications in the surface pattern were observed, indicating an additional coating on the GQDs (Fig. S1c). This can be associated with the coating of DNA probes on nanomaterials [23]. However, in the bio-recognition step with a positive sample for HPV16, a new morphological pattern related to the arrangement of the sample on the surface appeared (Fig. S1d). UV–vis characterization of GQDs was also carried out, which showed an absorbance peak between 325 – 375 nm (Fig. S1e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the self-assembly steps
ce:section.ce:para: All modification and biosensor construction steps were characterized using CV and DPV. This resulted in obtaining electrochemical patterns for each modification step (Fig. 3 ). In the CV and DPV tests, it can be observed that the unmodified carbon electrode displayed a voltammetric pattern with clear oxidation peaks (Fig. 3a and 3b). When evaluating the optimal concentration for Cys immobilization, it was observed that as the concentration increased, the oxidation peak increased in the DPV assay (Fig. S2). The concentration of 40 mM was chosen as ideal because it presents the highest oxidation peak. Modification of the carbon electrode surface with Cys led to an increase in the catalytic behavior of the surface. This behavior was noticeable in both CV (Fig. 3a) and DPV (Fig. 3b). The coating of the carbon electrode with the Cys layer occurs due to the cationic property that the amine group of cysteine can assume. This happens because cationic radicals enable the formation of nitrogen-carbon bonds [25]. During the suggested adsorption of Cys, cyclic voltammetry characterization may lead to the oxidation of Cys, generating cystine. This is reflected in the increase in the voltammetric peaks in the studies due to the cysteine oxidation process. Cystine is characterized by bonding two cysteine residues linked by disulfide bridges (S-S) [26]. This structure is related to the behavior observed in the DPV study, where a shift in the maximum anodic current peak to more positive potentials can be observed (Fig. 3b). This occurs because cystine tends to undergo oxidation at more positive potentials than cysteine due to the need for more energy to break the disulfide bridges present in cystine compared to the sulfhydryl (S-H) bonds present in cysteine [26,27].

As the second step in the biosensor self-assembly, GQDs were immobilized on the Cys layer through covalent bonding by using coupling agents (EDC/NHS). In the study of the ideal immobilization time of GQDs, a decrease in Ipa was observed over time. This reflects the covalent immobilization formed on the activated surface (Fig. S3). The ideal time was established at ten minutes due to the significant drop in the amperometric response, but without loss of signal in the other assembly stages (Fig. 3a and 3b). This behavior could be attributed to the hindrance of the electrocatalytic reaction of cysteine/cystine, along with the higher content of negatively charged carboxylic groups and hydroxyls in GQDs [17,28].

Subsequently, DNA probes were immobilized on the Cys-GQD layers through covalent bonding between GQDs and DNA probe. This bonding occurs due to chemical modification at the end of the probe with an NH2 group and the COOH groups on the GQDs' surface, activated by EDC/NHS. The characteristic electrochemical behavior was a decrease in the voltammetric responses due to the phosphate negative charge of the DNA (Fig. 3a and 3b) [29]. The Ipa values corresponding to each assembly step can be found in Table S1.

To validate the E6E7 biosensor, studies were conducted using plasmid samples of different HPV types at the same concentration, categorized as high risk (HPV16, 18, 33, and 45) and low risk (HPV06). Upon exposing the sensor to HPV samples, a decrease in the amperometric response was observed (Fig. 4 a and 4b). This phenomenon is related to the formation of the DNA double helix between the DNA probe acting as the bioreceptor and the sample acting as the analyte. The hybridization complex formation increases negative charges on the sensor's surface, decreasing the electron transfer. Furthermore, in this biodetection process, the blocking of the sensor surface also occurs, increasing the process of electrostatic repulsion. It is also worth noting that this process leads to a modification of the electrical density of the sensor surface and the electric double layer [30].

In this study, it was observed different responses between high and low-risk types. This behavior is related to the higher expression of the E6 and E7 proteins in oncogenic HPV genotypes [4,31]. These proteins can be identified even during the initial virus infection process. In addition, the difference in the electrochemical hybridization response can also be attributed to the conformational characteristics of the degenerate DNA probe, which allows binding to different types of HPV. Additionally, the remaining sequence of plasmids that do not participate in the hybridization site influences the blocking of the sensor surface, increasing the process of electrostatic repulsion. To assess the bioanalytical performance of the microfluidic E6E7 biosensor, a calibration curve was constructed using HPV33 plasmids at different concentrations. As the plasmid concentration increased, there was a decrease in the amperometric response due to the greater formation of the hybridization complex on the sensor surface (Fig. 4c and 4d). For biorecognition assessment, the ΔI% was calculated using the equation: ΔI (%) = [(IBIOSENSOR–ISAMPLE) / IBIOSENSOR] × 100. The ΔI% is directly proportional to the concentration of the analyte under evaluation. The linear regression study revealed an excellent response linearity with an R2 of 0.99 and the linear equation y = 86.08 + 5.66 log (×). Furthermore, the microfluidic E6E7 biosensor exhibited a limit of detection (LOD) of 26 fM and a limit of quantification (LQ) of 79.6 fM.

The specificity test was performed under the same conditions as the positive tests. However, we used a pool of non-complementary cDNA samples with Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) and Candida albicans genetic content. Electrochemical characterization revealed no significant electrochemical changes indicative of biorecognition, demonstrating the biosensor's specificity (Fig. 6b).

The microfluidic E6E7 biosensor was evaluated using suspensions of clinical cervical scraping samples from patients confirmed to have HPV through molecular techniques. During the experiments, cell samples were suspended in PBS and vigorously agitated to rupture the cells and expose the genetic material. Upon exposure to the samples, there was a decrease in the amperometric response proportional to the increase in concentration for all evaluated HPV types (HPV6, 11, 16, and 18) (Fig. 5
). When assessing the ΔI% from the biorecognition studies, differential responses were observed between high-risk and low-risk HPVs. Furthermore, the biosensor exhibited an excellent linear response for all evaluated samples (Fig. 6a). This is observed in Fig. 6, where HPV16 showed a more pronounced amperometric response despite being at the same concentration and condition as the other samples. However, there was a more pronounced response from HPV16 in concentrated dilutions as compared to HPV16 and HPV18. HPV16 is one of the main genotypes found in cases of cervical cancer worldwide [32]. Then, we observed that the response is related to the oncogenic potential of the virus.

Secondly, HPV18 displayed a high biorecognition response, corroborating global epidemiological findings that identify HPV 16 and 18 as the main genotypes found in cervical cancer [31]. The microfluidic E6E7 biosensor presented the following differential response regarding detection intensity: HPV16 >HPV18 >HPV6 >HPV11. Therefore, determining the presence and intensity of the E6 and E7 oncogenes can be of utmost importance in clinical decision-making for HPV treatment, even in the early stages of infection. This is because determining the viral genotype is crucial in treating cervical lesions, affecting the prognosis of patients [4,32].

Table 1 shows a comprehensive comparison among studies employing carbon electrodes in genosensors for HPV detection, it is evident that the present work has achieved good analytical sensitivity. Additionally, it offers advantages such as a rapid isothermal hybridization process and label-free signal detection. Moreover, the cited studies focus on the detection of HR-HPV; however, they do not provide the capability to differentiate between HPV types and have not conducted hybridization tests on direct patient samples.

For the reliability of any diagnostic test, the evaluation of repeatability and reproducibility is essential. Repeatability is related to the consistency of measurements when assessing the same sample at different times. At the same time, reproducibility deals with the proximity of results obtained using the same method with varying conditions [36]. Different biosensors on different days were employed to assess reproducibility in the E6E7 biosensor. Through this study, the microfluidic E6E7 biosensor demonstrated good reproducibility of 1.97 % and repeatability of 0.455 %, showcasing its bioanalytical capability.

Identifying the HPV genotype is crucial, even in the early stages of infection. The microfluidic E6E7 biosensor has demonstrated its ability to detect and differentiate between LR-HPV and HR-HPV, identifying oncogenes associated with the carcinogenic transformation process. The Cys-GQDs matrix has played a vital role in immobilizing the DNA probe, providing an organic and biocompatible environment. A biosensor with excellent signal reproducibility and repeatability was obtained. Moreover, the utilization of authentic clinical samples, coupled with a streamlined treatment and detection methodology, has significantly amplified the impact and credibility of the findings presented. Furthermore, the LOC genosensor developed in this study successfully distinguished between different HPV types and determined the expression levels of oncoproteins E6 and E7, which are crucial for cervical cancer development. The E6E7 microfluidic biosensor is an innovative and efficient tool for screening and detecting HPV oncogenes, offering a high level of bioanalytical performance.

Léony S. Oliveira: . Sanket Goel: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Khairunnisa Amreen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Norma Lucena-Silva: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Maria D.L. Oliveira: . Cesar A.S. Andrade: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support from the Brazilian National Council for Scientific and Technological Development/CNPq (grant number 304678/2021-0 and 304680/2021-4) and State Funding Agency of Pernambuco/FACEPE 29/2022 (APQ-1037-2.01/20). Léony S. Oliveira would like to thank CAPES and CNPq for the scholarship.
